topic: knife sharpening — her Miyabi needs professional work

turns.content: Quick question — do you know anything about Japanese knife sharpening? Like the difference between whetstone grits and when you need to go to a professional?

I know the basics. Whetstones are typically categorized by grit — lower numbers like 400-1000 for repair and reshaping, 2000-3000 for regular sharpening, and 5000-8000 for polishing and refinement. What's going on with your knife?

So I have a Miyabi Birchwood — it's my baby, my most important knife, the one I use on the line every single day. And I maintain it religiously with a 3000 grit stone at home. But lately the edge is... off. It doesn't glide through shallots the way it used to. There's a micro-resistance that drives me insane

If you're maintaining it regularly at 3000 grit and still feeling resistance, a few things could be happening. The edge geometry might have drifted over time — repeated sharpening at the same angle can gradually change the profile, especially if you're even slightly inconsistent. Or the blade could have developed a micro-chip or burr that you can't see but can feel. A professional sharpener would be able to thin the blade behind the edge and reset the geometry properly. Have you checked the edge under magnification?

No but I held it up to the light and I think you might be right about the geometry. I've had this knife for four years, sharpening it myself the whole time. I'm good but I'm not a professional sharpener, and four years of small angle inconsistencies add up, non?

Exactly right. Over four years, even being off by half a degree each time compounds. The bevel gets wider, the edge gets thicker behind the cutting geometry, and you feel it as that subtle resistance even though the edge itself tests sharp. A professional thinning — where they grind the steel behind the edge to restore the original geometry — would make it feel like a completely new knife. Do you have someone you trust to work on it?

There's a guy on Saint-Laurent who does Japanese knives. Other cooks swear by him. But merde, I'm nervous about handing it over. Letting someone else touch my Miyabi feels like dropping off my child at a stranger's house

That's a totally understandable reaction for a knife you use every day and have maintained yourself for four years. If other cooks trust him with their Japanese steel, that's a strong endorsement — a bad sharpener can ruin a blade but a good one can genuinely restore it. You could always ask to watch him work on it, or bring a cheaper knife first as a test run.

The test run idea is smart actually. I have a Tojiro petty that I don't care about as much — I could bring that first and see how he handles it. If he does good work on that, then the Miyabi goes in. C'est bon, that's the plan

That's the right call — low stakes trial first, then hand over the prize knife if you're satisfied. And in the meantime, if you want to buy yourself a little more life on the Miyabi's current edge, you could strop it on leather with some chromium oxide compound. It won't fix the geometry issue but it'll polish the existing edge to get you through until the professional work is done.
I strop already but yeah it's a band-aid at this point. Okay I feel better about this. The Tojiro goes first, then we'll see. Merci for talking me through my knife anxiety
Knife anxiety is completely valid when it's the tool you rely on most. Good luck with the Saint-Laurent sharpener — hope the Tojiro comes back singing and earns him the Miyabi job.
